audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/teamviewer.git to /tmp/aur-sleuth/aur-sleuth-5rmaiord...
Cloned repository to /tmp/aur-sleuth/aur-sleuth-5rmaiord
Analyzing teamviewer AUR package (working in /tmp/aur-sleuth/aur-sleuth-5rmaiord) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines standard VCS-free sources: it downloads prebuilt `.deb` packages directly from `dl.teamviewer.com`, which is TeamViewer's official distribution domain. The URLs are constructed using `pkgver`, but this does not involve arbitrary command execution or dynamic content fetching from untrusted locations—it remains within the expected behavior of a source definition. There is no top-level code execution, command substitution, or `pkgver()` function override; the `pkgver` is hard-coded, so no external calls occur when sourcing the PKGBUILD.

The `source` arrays use string interpolation with `${pkgver%%.*}x`, which safely extracts the major version (e.g., "15" from "15.78.3") to build the correct download URL. This is a common and safe practice in PKGBUILDs. All `sha256sums` are provided and match the sources, ensuring integrity during download. No code is executed outside of the build functions (`prepare()`, `package()`), and those are not run under `--nobuild --nodeps --noprepare --printsrcinfo`.

Thus, simply sourcing the PKGBUILD and downloading the sources poses no risk of remote code execution, data exfiltration, or unauthorized network activity beyond the expected and trusted TeamViewer CDN.
</details>
<summary>
Safe source retrieval from official domain with verified checksums</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Safe source retrieval from official domain with verified checksums[/]
Running makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare to download sources for agent...
makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare successful
Running makepkg --printsrcinfo to get source files for agent...
makepkg --printsrcinfo successful
Reviewing required files...
[0/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
[0/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD, teamviewer.install...
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD fetches TeamViewer binaries from the official TeamViewer download domain (dl.teamviewer.com) over HTTPS for multiple architectures, using pinned SHA-256 checksums, which ensures integrity. The source URLs are parameterized with `pkgver` in a standard and expected way, and no untrusted or obfuscated network requests are present.

The `prepare()` function unpacks the .deb data tarball and patches a script to accommodate Arch Linux's Qt5 Quick Controls installation path. This patch uses a straightforward `sed` command to insert a conditional check that avoids false negatives in dependency detection. The patching is transparent and targets a legitimate compatibility issue. It also runs `teamviewer_setup checklibs`, which is a diagnostic script included in the upstream package, not a downloaded or dynamically generated payload.

The `package()` function installs unpacked files to standard system directories, sets up systemd services, and removes unnecessary components like `libdepend` on x86_64 to avoid pulling in 32-bit dependencies unnecessarily—this is a common optimization in Arch packaging. No unexpected file operations, network calls, obfuscated code, or data exfiltration mechanisms are present. All actions are consistent with standard AUR packaging practices for redistributing upstream binaries.
</details>
<summary>
Standard packaging of upstream binary, no malicious code</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing teamviewer.install...
+ Reviewed PKGBUILD. Status: SAFE -- Standard packaging of upstream binary, no malicious code
LLM auditresponse for teamviewer.install:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided `teamviewer.install` script contains standard post-installation, post-upgrade, and post-removal hooks typical of Arch Linux packages. The `post_install` function displays informational messages instructing the user to enable the TeamViewer daemon via systemd, which is a legitimate requirement for TeamViewer functionality. This does not execute any commands automatically nor performs any unauthorized actions. The `post_upgrade` function simply reuses the installation message, which is a common practice. The `post_remove` function removes the `/opt/teamviewer` directory upon package removal, which is expected clean-up behavior for files installed outside standard hierarchies.

There are no network operations, obfuscated code, or suspicious command executions. All operations are transparent, minimal, and within the expected scope of an install script for a package that installs software into `/opt`. The use of `rm -rf` is confined to a package-specific directory and does not target system-critical paths. No `eval`, `curl`, `wget`, or other high-risk constructs are present. The script does not exfiltrate data or introduce backdoors.

While the script assumes `/opt/teamviewer` is fully owned by the package (which should be ensured in the PKGBUILD), this is not inherently unsafe. Overall, the script adheres to standard AUR packaging practices and presents no evidence of malicious behavior.
</details>
<summary>
Standard install script, no malicious behavior detected</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/2] Reviewing ...

Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines architecture-specific sources that point to official TeamViewer Linux downloads over HTTPS from `dl.teamviewer.com`, a domain associated with the legitimate vendor. The URLs use string interpolation with `$pkgver` and `${pkgver%%.*}` to construct versioned paths, which is a standard practice and does not introduce remote code execution risk during source fetching. The `sha256sums` are explicitly listed and match the expected values for the corresponding `.deb` files, ensuring integrity at download time.

During the execution of `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo`, only the global scope and `pkgver()` function (if present) would be evaluated. In this case, `pkgver` is a static variable and no command substitution or function call is used in the top-level scope. The `prepare()` and `package()` functions are not executed due to `--noprepare` and `--nobuild`, so their contents are out of scope for this narrow gate. No network requests beyond downloading the declared sources occur, and no data exfiltration or dynamic code evaluation happens when sourcing the PKGBUILD.

While the use of `warning` messages in `prepare()` and `package()` suggests potential manual intervention issues, they do not impact the safety of the current commands. Overall, sourcing the PKGBUILD and fetching the sources poses no risk of executing malicious payloads or leaking sensitive data.
</details>
<summary>
No unsafe operations during source fetch and parse
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD fetches TeamViewer binaries from the official TeamViewer download domain (dl.teamviewer.com) over HTTPS for supported architectures (x86_64, armv7h, aarch64). The sources are version-pinned Debian packages with matching SHA-256 checksums, ensuring integrity. The prepare() function unpacks the data.tar.* archives from the .deb files and applies a benign patch to `teamviewer_setup` to skip a Qt Quick Controls check by using a local filesystem test, which is specific to Arch Linux's directory layout. It also runs `teamviewer_setup checklibs`, which is part of the upstream's dependency verification and logs results but does not alter system behavior beyond diagnosis.

The package() function installs unpacked files into the package directory, removes unnecessary components (like /etc/apt and libdepend on x86_64 to avoid pulling in 32-bit libraries unnecessarily), and sets up systemd service files and license symlinks. All operations are scoped to the build and package directory, with no evidence of unauthorized network activity, code execution, or data exfiltration. The use of `sed` to patch a setup script is transparent and documented. No obfuscated commands, unexpected downloads, or post-install execution are present.

While the package disables stripping and includes binary blobs (expected for proprietary software), these are consistent with TeamViewer's upstream distribution model. There are no signs of injected backdoors, telemetry activation beyond what the upstream application may include, or supply-chain tampering. The build logic follows standard Arch packaging practices for closed-source software.
</details>
<summary>
Standard packaging for proprietary remote access tool</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Reviewed PKGBUILD. Status: SAFE -- Standard packaging for proprietary remote access tool
LLM auditresponse for teamviewer.install:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The `teamviewer.install` script contains standard AUR package lifecycle functions: `post_install`, `post_upgrade`, and `post_remove`. The `post_install` function displays a message instructing the user to enable the TeamViewer daemon via systemctl, which is expected guidance for proper functionality. The `post_upgrade` function simply calls `post_install`, a common practice to avoid code duplication. The `post_remove` function removes the `/opt/teamviewer` directory during uninstallation, which is appropriate cleanup for files installed under `/opt`.

There are no network operations, no execution of downloaded code, and no use of dangerous constructs such as `eval` or `base64`. The operations performed are limited to informational output and removal of the application's own installation directory, both within expected packaging norms. The script does not access or exfiltrate user data, nor does it modify system files beyond what is necessary for the application's operation.

This script adheres to standard and safe AUR packaging practices. While it removes files in `post_remove`, it does so in a targeted manner within `/opt`, which is the standard location for such software. No system-wide or user data directories (e.g., `/home`, `~/.config`) are touched beyond the app's own path.
</details>
<summary>
Standard install script, no malicious behavior
</summary>
</security_assessment>

<security_assessment>

<details>
The provided file is a Debian control file for the `teamviewer` package, which describes metadata such as package name, version, dependencies, maintainer, and description. It contains no executable code, scripts, or embedded payloads. The content consists entirely of standard Debian packaging fields with no obfuscation, suspicious commands, or network operations.

All dependencies and recommendations are system libraries or standard components necessary for GUI and system integration, consistent with TeamViewer's functionality. The `Homepage` and licensing information reference official TeamViewer domains and policies. There is no evidence of code injection, backdoors, or supply-chain tampering in this file.

While TeamViewer as an application has privacy implications due to telemetry and remote access capabilities, those are upstream design choices and not signs of a malicious AUR package modification. This file does not introduce additional risks beyond the known behavior of the official software.
</details>
<summary>

<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `conffiles` lists configuration files that are part of the package, specifically `/etc/apt/sources.list.d/teamviewer.list`. This is a standard Debian/Ubuntu mechanism for managing APT repository sources, and including it in `conffiles` ensures that the file is preserved during package upgrades, preventing accidental overwrites by the package manager.

This behavior is consistent with standard packaging practices for APT-based distributions. The presence of a teamviewer.list entry suggests the package configures an external repository, which is typical for TeamViewer to provide automatic updates. While this may raise privacy or trust considerations (as the repository is controlled by TeamViewer), it does not constitute a supply-chain attack or malicious behavior in the context of the AUR packaging.

No execution, obfuscation, or unexpected network activity occurs in this file. It merely declares a configuration file path.
</details>
<summary>

LLM auditresponse for com.teamviewer.TeamViewer.Daemon.conf:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `com.teamviewer.TeamViewer.Daemon.conf` is a D-Bus configuration file used to define system-wide message bus permissions for the TeamViewer daemon. It grants the root user the right to own the D-Bus service `com.teamviewer.TeamViewer.Daemon` and allows default system clients to send messages to this service via the `com.teamviewer.TeamViewer.IpcAuthority` interface. This is standard D-Bus policy configuration for system services that require inter-process communication (IPC) and privileged operation.

The configuration does not include any executable code, network operations, file manipulations, or obfuscated content. It follows D-BUS DTD standards and only defines access control policies expected for a remote desktop application like TeamViewer, which needs system-level access for device control and user session management. These permissions are scoped strictly to the declared service and interface, with no evidence of excessive or suspicious privileges.

Such files are routinely installed by system services needing D-Bus integration (e.g., NetworkManager, BlueZ, PulseAudio), and this one aligns with legitimate TeamViewer functionality. There is no indication of malicious injection or supply chain compromise in this file.
</details>
<summary>
Standard D-Bus config for a system service.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

The provided file is a D-BUS service configuration file (`com.teamviewer.TeamViewer.service`) that defines how the TeamViewer service should be activated via D-BUS. It specifies the D-BUS name `com.teamviewer.TeamViewer` and sets the executable path to `/opt/teamviewer/tv_bin/TeamViewer`, which is the standard installation path for TeamViewer binaries.

This file does not contain executable code, scripts, or network operations. It is a declarative configuration file used by the D-BUS daemon to launch the TeamViewer application when a D-BUS method call is made to that service name. Such files are standard components of desktop applications that integrate with the D-BUS system for inter-process communication.

There are no signs of tampering, obfuscation, or malicious redirection. The `Exec` path points to the legitimate, expected location for TeamViewer's binary. No environment variables are manipulated, no unexpected file operations occur, and no remote resources are accessed. This file aligns with standard practice for systemd or D-BUS-integrated desktop applications.
</details>

It specifies the service name `com.teamviewer.TeamViewer.Desktop` and the executable path `/opt/teamviewer/tv_bin/TeamViewer_Desktop`, which is consistent with TeamViewer's installation layout.

This file does not contain executable code, scripts, or commands that could lead to arbitrary code execution, data exfiltration, or unauthorized network activity. It is a declarative configuration file used by the D-Bus system to launch the TeamViewer desktop component when requested by a client. The `Exec` path points to a known binary within the TeamViewer installation directory, which is expected behavior for applications that expose D-Bus interfaces.

While the upstream TeamViewer application may have privacy implications due to its remote access functionality, this file itself does not introduce any additional security risk beyond the intended operation of the software. It does not deviate from standard D-Bus service file practices.
</details>

The service is set to restart on failure, with reasonable rate-limiting parameters.

All directives used in the unit file are standard and consistent with typical packaging practices for a background service. The ExecStart path points to a binary within the package's own installation directory (`/opt/teamviewer`), which is normal for proprietary applications like TeamViewer. There are no network downloads, obfuscated commands, suspicious file operations, or invocation of dangerous scripts.

While TeamViewer as an application has privacy implications due to its remote access functionality (including potential telemetry), this service file does not introduce additional malicious behavior. It merely configures the legitimate upstream daemon to run under systemd. The file aligns with expected functionality and poses no evidence of supply-chain compromise.

LLM auditresponse for tv-delayed-start.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The script `tv-delayed-start.sh` is part of the TeamViewer package and is designed to delay the startup of TeamViewer's daemon until network connectivity and essential D-Bus services (specifically D-Bus system bus and logind) are available. It performs standard system checks using common tools like `ip`, `dbus-send`, and `command -v`. The functions `WaitForNetwork` and `WaitForServices` implement retry loops with delays, waiting for network interfaces with IPv4 addresses (excluding loopback) and for D-Bus-managed system services to become ready.

There is no evidence of malicious behavior. The script does not download or execute external code, exfiltrate data, or perform unauthorized file system modifications. It writes a simple timing log to a local file under `$TV_BASE_DIR/logfiles/`, which is a legitimate diagnostic practice. The use of D-Bus commands to check and start system services is appropriate for a system-level application like TeamViewer that integrates with system daemons. All operations are transparent, well-documented in intent, and consistent with the expected behavior of a delayed startup script for a remote access service.

Some hygiene notes: the script uses unquoted command substitutions in `[ -x $(command -v ...) ]`, which is fragile, and `cmdExists` is defined but not consistently used. However, these are coding style issues, not security vulnerabilities. No obfuscation, command injection, or supply-chain attack vectors are present.
</details>
<summary>
Legitimate startup script with no malicious code
</summary>
</security_assessment>

<security_assessment>

The PKGBUILD defines sources for three architectures (x86_64, armv7h, aarch64) from the official TeamViewer download domain (`dl.teamviewer.com`), with pinned SHA256 checksums. No global-scope code, `pkgver()` function, or command substitutions execute malicious operations. The `source` array is fetched over HTTPS, and the checksums are provided (not SKIP). There is no obfuscated code, no unexpected network requests, and no data exfiltration. The `prepare()`, `build()`, and `package()` functions are not executed during the `--nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` step, so their content is out of scope. Running `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo` is safe.
</details>
<summary>Standard PKGBUILD with pinned checksums from official source.</summary>
</security_assessment>

<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD downloads official TeamViewer .deb packages from the vendor's domain (dl.teamviewer.com) over HTTPS, with pinned SHA256 checksums.  The prepare() function extracts the archive, patches the upstream `teamviewer_setup` script with a simple sed substitution to adjust QtQuickControls detection for Arch paths, and runs the upstream's own dependency checker — all routine packaging steps.  There is no obfuscated code, no unexpected network calls, no exfiltration of local data, and no execution of code from untrusted origins.  The package() function copies the extracted files into the package directory, removes Debian-specific `/etc/apt`, installs a systemd service, and creates a license symlink.  These operations are standard for converting a .deb into an Arch package and do not exhibit malicious behavior.
</details>
<summary>No evidence of malicious code or supply chain attack.</summary>
